meta.title: The Daily Reflection Tree
meta.description: A deterministic end-of-day reflection tool walking employees through Locus, Orientation, and Radius — the three axes of psychological growth.

nodes.text:
Good evening. You made it to the end of another day — that alone means something.

This is a quiet space. No performance reviews here. No judgment. Just a few questions to help you see today a little more clearly.

Shall we begin?
First: if today were a weather report, what would it be?

options.label: ☀️  Clear skies — I felt on top of things
🌤  Partly cloudy — some wins, some friction
⛈  Stormy — things kept going sideways
🌫  Foggy — I'm not sure what happened today

nodes.text: A "{A1_OPEN.answer}" kind of day. When something went well today, what was the honest reason?

options.label: I prepared for it and it paid off
The team or circumstances aligned in my favour
I adapted when my original plan didn't work
I'm not entirely sure — it just worked out

nodes.text: A "{A1_OPEN.label}" kind of day. When something went wrong, what was your first instinct?

options.label: Figure out what was still in my control
Wait for someone else to fix it or unblock me
Push through alone without asking for help
Feel stuck and unsure what to do next

nodes.text: Think of one specific moment today where you felt the most in control of how things went. What did you actually do?

options.label: I made a decision others were avoiding
I asked for what I needed instead of waiting
I changed my approach when the first one failed
I set a boundary or said no to something

nodes.text: Even on a day where things felt out of your hands — was there one small choice you made that was yours alone?

options.label: Yes — I chose how I responded to a setback
Yes — I chose to keep going instead of giving up
Maybe — I'm struggling to identify it right now
Honestly, no — today really was outside my control

nodes.text:
You said "{A1_Q2_INTERNAL.label}" — that's not small. Agency isn't about controlling outcomes. It's about staying in the driver's seat regardless of road conditions.

Today you drove. That's worth noticing.

You chose how you responded — even on a hard day. That's actually the core of agency: not that things went well, but that you made a call when it counted.

That's your footprint in the day. Even on the difficult ones.

Some days genuinely are hard — the constraint isn't always inside us. That's real. 

But here's a gentle question to carry: between the moment something went wrong and the moment you reacted — was there any gap, however small, where you had a choice?

Most days, there is. Tomorrow, try catching it.

You've just examined how you showed up as an agent in your own day.

Now let's shift the lens: from how you handled things — to what you gave.
Think about the people you interacted with today. Which of these feels truest?

options.label: I mostly focused on getting my own work done well
I noticed someone struggling and did something about it
I felt my contributions weren't being seen or credited
I invested time in someone else's growth, not just mine

nodes.text: That's generous. When you helped or invested in someone today — what motivated you, honestly?

options.label: It felt like the right thing to do — I didn't overthink it
I knew it would help the team's shared goal
I hoped they'd return the favour at some point
I wanted to be seen as a good colleague

nodes.text: Fair. But let's look closely: was there a moment today where you could have helped someone — and chose not to, or it simply didn't cross your mind?

options.label: Yes — I was too busy and I know it
Yes — it crossed my mind but I hesitated
Not that I noticed, but maybe I'm not seeing it
No — I really did do what I could today

nodes.text: One more: was there anything today you did that no one asked for, no one will necessarily notice, but you did it anyway because it was the right thing?

options.label: Yes — and I'm okay that it went unnoticed
Yes — but honestly, I was hoping someone would notice
Not today — but it's something I do try to do
I'm not sure I think that way yet

nodes.text: There's something honest in that. Recognition matters — we're human. But: what would it mean to give something today that had no audience?

options.label: It would feel complete — contribution doesn't need witnesses
Hard, honestly — I need some form of acknowledgement
I do this sometimes, but not as a default
I've never really thought about it that way

nodes.text: Here's a reframe: think of one person from today who had a harder day than you. What do you know about what they were dealing with?

options.label: A lot — I'm aware of what they're going through
A little — I've caught glimpses
Not much — I tend to stay in my lane
I didn't think about anyone else's day today

nodes.text:
Giving without keeping score is rarer than it sounds. Most people give and then — quietly, almost automatically — start tracking.

The fact that you noticed it, did it anyway, and can hold it without needing a receipt? That's what teams are built on.

Wanting recognition is human. And you're already doing something important — you're questioning the *why* behind what you give.

That space between "I gave" and "because I wanted to" — that's where real contribution grows. You're already looking at it.

Entitlement isn't about being selfish — it's usually just tunnel vision. You're focused, locked in, doing your thing.

But organisations breathe through the spaces between job descriptions — the quiet help, the unrequested assist. Tomorrow: notice one person before you think about yourself. Just once.

Good. You've looked at yourself as an agent, and as a contributor.

One final shift: from what today meant for *you* — to what today meant for *others*.
When you think back to today's most significant moment — who's in the picture?

options.label: Just me — it was a personal challenge or milestone
Me and one other person
My team or a group I'm part of
Someone who relies on what we produce — a customer, a user, someone downstream

nodes.text: Personal challenges carry real weight. But in the ripple of that moment — was there anyone who was affected by how you handled it, even indirectly?

options.label: Yes — my mood or stress affected the people around me
Yes — my output or decision affected a colleague's work
Possibly, but I didn't stop to think about it
No — this was genuinely just about me

nodes.text: You're thinking about others — that's a wider lens already. In that shared moment, what were you most concerned with?

options.label: Making sure we reached a good outcome together
How I was being perceived in the interaction
What the other person actually needed
How this would affect me going forward

nodes.text: You're thinking about the downstream impact — that's a wide frame. What does it feel like to work toward something for people you may never meet?

options.label: Meaningful — it's why I do this work
Abstract — I believe it but don't feel it day to day
Honest answer: I rarely think about it that far out
I'm not sure this work connects to anything that large

nodes.text:
There's nothing wrong with a day that's about you. Self-awareness is its own form of radius.

But here's a thread to pull: *you* exist inside a web. Your energy, your presence, your output — all of it touches someone. Tomorrow, try to hold just one other person's experience alongside yours. Not instead of — alongside.

You're starting to see the web — that your choices and moods extend outward, beyond your own story.

That awareness is the first act of transcendence. You don't need to fix everyone. You just need to *see* them. Today, you did.

You moved toward what the other person needed, or what you needed together. That's a meaningful act — more than most people manage consistently.

The radius you hold is already wider than average. The question is: can you extend it one level further — to people you haven't met yet?

Working toward people you'll never meet is the highest form of contribution — and also one of the most reliable sources of sustained meaning.

Maslow called it self-transcendence. He argued it was the peak of human development — not achievement, not belonging, but service to something beyond the self.

You're already practicing it.
Before we close: if you could carry one thing from today — one moment, one decision, one insight — into tomorrow, what kind of thing would it be?

options.label: A reminder that I have more control than I think
A specific person I want to show up better for
A choice I'd make differently next time
The feeling of having genuinely contributed something

nodes.text:
Here's what today looked like:

On **Agency** (how you drove your day): you leaned {axis1.dominant}.
On **Contribution** (what you gave): you leaned {axis2.dominant}.
On **Radius** (how far your concern reached): you leaned {axis3.dominant}.

You said today felt like "{A1_OPEN.label}" — and you're walking away choosing to carry "{A3_Q_FINAL.label}".

That gap between how the day *felt* and what you're choosing to *take from it* — that's growth. Every single time.

{summary_reflection}

summary_reflections.internal_contribution_other: Three for three today. You saw your agency, gave without keeping score, and thought beyond yourself. That's not a perfect day — it's a grounded one.
summary_reflections.internal_contribution_self: Strong on agency and contribution. The next edge: extending your radius a little further outward. You're already close.
summary_reflections.internal_entitlement_other: You drove your day and kept others in your frame. The middle axis — what you gave freely — is worth revisiting tomorrow.
summary_reflections.internal_entitlement_self: You're clear on your own agency — that's your strongest asset. Tomorrow: one moment of giving with no agenda, and one moment of seeing someone else's picture.
summary_reflections.external_contribution_other: You gave generously and kept a wide frame — even on a day that didn't feel entirely in your control. That's resilience wearing a quiet face.
summary_reflections.external_contribution_self: You contributed meaningfully today. The agency question is worth sitting with — not to blame yourself, but to find where the handle is next time.
summary_reflections.external_entitlement_other: A hard day — things felt out of control and the giving was harder than usual. But you're still here, still looking. That's what matters.
summary_reflections.external_entitlement_self: Today was genuinely tough. Three axes to work on is just information, not a verdict. Every day is a new tree. Tomorrow, try just one: find one moment where you had the wheel.

nodes.text: See you tomorrow. You showed up today — that's always the first step.
